policy_statement: Implement Government Regulation No. 46/2017 on Environmental Economic Instruments.
mechanism_description: Implementing environmental economic instruments uses price signals and incentives to shift activities toward lower-emission options, supporting broader emissions reductions.
primary_causal_pathway: Price signal/incentives → behavior & investment shift → ↓ fossil fuel use/↓ emitting activities → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Government applies economy-wide or sectoral economic instruments (taxes, fees, permit trading, subsidy removal, targeted incentives) that increase the cost of emissions-intensive choices and/or decrease the cost of low-carbon alternatives.  
(2) Mediating physical/economic process: Altered relative prices drive demand reduction for carbon-intensive goods, fuel switching, efficiency improvements, and technology adoption; firms internalize the external cost of emissions and optimize to reduce taxable/regulated emissions.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in GHG emissions across covered sectors (and potentially accelerated low-carbon investment), lowering cumulative emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: By shifting prices and incentives economy-wide, these instruments most strongly drive reduced fossil-fuel use and other emitting activities, resulting in lower total GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Economic price signals/incentives → changes in production/consumption and investment → lower emitting activity → ↓ GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although instruments may improve resource efficiency or fund resilience, their direct and largest climate effect—common to the mechanism—is reducing emissions via internalizing carbon/pollution costs and incentivizing abatement.  
3) Edge cases applied: If an “environmental economic instrument” were designed purely for non-climate objectives (e.g., litter fees) with negligible GHG impact, it would not fit this canonical mechanism; likewise, revenue recycling into adaptation is secondary unless the instrument’s main intent/effect is hazard reduction.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy genuinely implementing environmental economic instruments for broad emissions reductions shares the same dominant mitigation pathway of price-driven abatement and technology shift.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific instrument type (tax vs ETS vs subsidy reform), sectoral coverage, and whether co-benefits (air quality, revenue for adaptation, equity impacts) dominate in practice must be assessed at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Possible ambiguity if the instrument targets general environmental quality rather than GHGs, or if it is primarily a revenue instrument with weak emissions responsiveness; classification assumes the instrument meaningfully prices emissions or incentivizes low-carbon choices consistent with “economy-wide emissions reduction.”
key_indicators: “Government Regulation No. 46/2017”; “Environmental Economic Instrument”; “supported by a number of PaMs”
co_benefits: Potential non-climate environmental improvements (e.g., reduced local air/water pollution), but not specified in the text.
instance_edge_case_notes: The text names the regulation but does not specify which economic instrument(s) (tax/fee/trading/incentive) are used; classification is therefore at the generic pricing/economic-instrument level.
